name: Limits and Derivatives — The Idea of Calculus
whatIsIt: A LIMIT asks: what value does f(x) head towards as x gets very close to some number? A DERIVATIVE measures the instantaneous rate of change — the slope of the curve at a point. The key shortcut is the POWER RULE: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹. The derivative of a constant is 0, and constants multiply through: d/dx(k·xⁿ) = k·n·xⁿ⁻¹.
whyNeeded: Calculus is how we describe change precisely: instantaneous speed, the slope of a curve, how fast anything grows or shrinks. Derivatives power physics, economics, machine learning and engineering — anywhere a rate of change matters.
realLifeUses: Instant speed from a distance–time curve (speedometer)
Rate a population or investment is growing right now
The slope of a hill at a single point

vocabulary.meaning: The value f(x) approaches as x nears a chosen number, written lim(x→a) f(x).
The instantaneous rate of change / slope of the curve, written f′(x) or df/dx.
d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹ — bring the power down, reduce it by one.
How fast the output changes as the input changes.

story.title: The Speedometer Secret
story.text: In the car, Karan asks how the speedometer knows the speed at one instant, not the average. Chachu says, 'It measures the rate of change of distance — that's a derivative.' Later, given the curve y = x², Chachu asks its slope rule. Karan uses the power rule: d/dx(x²) = 2x. At x = 3 the slope is 2 × 3 = 6. 'That's how steep the curve is right there,' Chachu nods.
story.extractedProblem: Using the power rule, find the derivative of x², then its value at x = 3.
story.answerInStory: 6 — d/dx(x²) = 2x, and at x = 3 that is 2 × 3 = 6.

visual.caption: The derivative is the slope of the tangent line touching the curve. For y = x² the tangent at x = 3 has slope 2×3 = 6.

teachingGallery.title: Bring the power down
teachingGallery.note: Power rule: the exponent jumps to the front, then drops by one.

examples.caption: A limit: as x creeps toward 2, f(x) heads to its limiting value.

standardMethod.summary: For a simple limit of a continuous function, substitute the value. For derivatives use the power rule d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹, treat constants' derivative as 0, and keep multiplying constants.
steps: For lim(x→a) of a nice (continuous) function, just substitute x = a.
For a derivative, apply the power rule term by term: xⁿ → n·xⁿ⁻¹.
The derivative of a constant is 0.
A constant multiplier stays: d/dx(k·xⁿ) = k·n·xⁿ⁻¹.
To get the slope at a point, substitute that x into the derivative.
x² is continuous, so substitute x = 2.

workedExamples.problem: Differentiate x³ and give its value at x = 2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing d/dx(x²) = 2x² instead of 2x (forgetting to reduce the power).
commonMistakes.fix: The power rule brings the exponent down AND reduces it by one: x² → 2x¹ = 2x.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking the derivative of a constant like 7 is 7.
commonMistakes.fix: A constant never changes, so its rate of change is 0. d/dx(7) = 0.

easy.q: What is the derivative of the constant 10?

medium.q: d/dx(x²) = 2x. Its value at x = 5 is?
In d/dx(x⁵) = 5x⁴, what number comes to the front?
hintLadder: Power rule brings the exponent down.

challenge.q: d/dx(x³) = 3x². Its value at x = 2 is 3 × 4 = ?
A student writes d/dx(x²) = 2x². What's the fix?
hintLadder: Power rule lowers the exponent by 1.

questions.q: d/dx(x⁴) = 4x³. The number in front is?

teachBackPrompt: Explain to a grown-up that a derivative is the slope of a curve at a point (like a speedometer reading), and use the power rule to show d/dx(x²) = 2x, which is 6 at x = 3.

revisionCard.summary: Limit: value f(x) approaches as x → a (for nice functions, substitute). Derivative = instantaneous slope/rate of change. Power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹ (bring power down, reduce by 1). Derivative of a constant = 0. Slope at a point: substitute x into the derivative.

trickPractice.intro: Bring the power down, drop it by one. Rapid-fire the power rule and evaluate at a point.

rememberIt.hook: Derivative = instant rate of change = slope of the curve
rememberIt.unpack: Power rule: d/dx(x^n) = n x^(n-1) (bring the power down, drop it by 1). A limit is the value a function approaches as x nears a point.

studentQuestions.q: What does a derivative actually mean?
studentQuestions.a: How fast something changes at an instant - the slope of the curve at that exact point (like speed from a distance-time graph).
The power rule: for x^n, bring n to the front and reduce the power by 1. x^3 -> 3 x^2.

formulas.name: Power rule
formulas.remember: Bring the power down, drop it by 1

formulas.name: Product rule
formulas.remember: first x d(second) + second x d(first)
